items.headline: Mediterranean crossing deaths near 1,000 in first three months as arrivals fall sharply
items.body: The International Organization for Migration reports nearly 1,000 people have died crossing the Mediterranean in 2026, one of the deadliest starts to a year since record keeping began in 2014, despite arrivals to Italy dropping by a third. Fatalities are rising even as crossings decline, with inadequate search and rescue capacity leaving migrants on unseaworthy vessels to drown as they flee conflict and instability across North Africa.

items.headline: Pakistan mediates first direct Iran-United States peace talks as fragile ceasefire holds
items.body: Senior American and Iranian delegations begin negotiations in Islamabad tomorrow following a two-week ceasefire agreed this week that halted a 40-day war which killed thousands and shut the Strait of Hormuz. The talks represent Pakistan's emergence as an unlikely mediator in a conflict that has caused the largest energy supply disruption in history, with global oil flows down 20 percent and prices spiking above 100 dollars per barrel before retreating on ceasefire news.

items.headline: Benin votes Sunday in first leadership transition after decade of tightening democratic space
items.body: Eight million Beninese elect a new president on Sunday as incumbent Patrice Talon steps down after ten years in which opposition has been systematically excluded, with the main challenger barred from running for failing to secure parliamentary endorsements critics say were engineered to keep rivals out. The election follows a failed coup in December and tests whether electoral competition can survive in a West African coastal state surrounded by military juntas.

items.headline: Africa to outpace Asia in economic growth for first time as IMF forecasts 4.3 percent expansion
items.body: The International Monetary Fund projects African economies will grow faster than Asian economies in 2026 for the first time, driven by macroeconomic stabilization in key countries and high commodity prices, while China's structural deceleration drags down the Asian average. The shift reflects reforms in countries like Ghana and Ethiopia alongside demographic momentum, though much of Africa's growth stems from population increases rather than productivity gains, limiting per capita income improvements.
